era_context: The mid-13th century: the Mongol Empire under Möngke Khan at its height; Hulagu Khan's campaign against the Assassins and preparation for the conquest of Persia and Iraq; the Crusader states in decline; the Song Dynasty under increasing Mongol pressure; European feudalism and scholasticism flourishing.

events.title: Fall of Alamut and destruction of the Assassins' stronghold
events.description: In 1256, Hulagu Khan's Mongol forces captured and destroyed Alamut, the legendary fortress stronghold of the Nizari Ismaili Assassins in the Alborz Mountains of Persia. The fall of Alamut marked the effective end of the Assassins as a major political and military power. The fortress, which had been the center of Assassin power for nearly two centuries, was razed, and its library and archives were destroyed (though some sources suggest Hulagu's forces preserved some texts). The destruction of Alamut represented a turning point in medieval Islamic history and demonstrated Mongol military superiority over even the most formidable fortified positions.

sources.name: Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles)
Juvayni, Ta'rikh-i jahan-gusha (History of the World-Conqueror)
Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Alamut in 1256 is well-documented in multiple Islamic sources. Archaeological evidence from the site confirms the destruction. The date is precise.
cross_references: 1257-001 (Continuation of Hulagu's campaign against the Assassins)

events.title: Hulagu Khan's establishment of the Il-Khanate
events.description: In 1256, Hulagu Khan was formally established as the Il-Khan (subordinate khan) of the western Mongol territories, ruling over Persia, Iraq, and parts of the Levant and Anatolia. This marked the formal establishment of the Il-Khanate as a distinct Mongol khanate, separate from the Great Khan's direct rule but nominally subordinate to Möngke Khan. The Il-Khanate would become one of the four major Mongol successor states and would rule the region until the early 14th century.

sources.name: Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles)
Juvayni, Ta'rikh-i jahan-gusha (History of the World-Conqueror)

events.certainty_note: Hulagu's establishment as Il-Khan in 1256 is well-documented in Islamic sources. The date is precise.

events.title: Henry III of England and the Provisions of Oxford
events.description: In 1256, Henry III of England was consolidating his power following the Provisions of Oxford (1258, but preparations were underway in 1256). The period 1256–1258 saw increasing tension between Henry III and the English baronage over royal prerogatives and financial demands. In 1256, Henry was attempting to maintain royal authority while facing baronial opposition. This period would culminate in the Barons' War (1264–1267), but in 1256, the conflict was still in its early stages.
key_figures: Simon de Montfort (Earl of Leicester, baronial leader)

sources.name: Roger of Wendover, Flowers of History

events.certainty_note: The general political situation in England in 1256 is well-documented, but specific events in that year are less precisely recorded. The Provisions of Oxford were formally issued in 1258, not 1256.

events.title: Song Dynasty military campaigns and Mongol pressure
events.description: The Southern Song Dynasty, under Emperor Lizong (r. 1224–1264), continued to face Mongol military pressure from the north. In 1256, Kublai Khan's forces launched campaigns against Song territories, particularly in the Yangtze River valley. The Song maintained their defensive positions but were gradually losing territory. This period saw intensive military preparations and the development of advanced naval and fortification technologies as the Song attempted to preserve their realm.
key_figures: Kublai Khan (Mongol commander, future Great Khan)

sources.name: Song Shi (Official History of the Song Dynasty)
Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Song-Mongol conflict is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 1256 are less precisely dated. The sources are compilations written after the Song's fall in 1279.

events.title: Alexander IV and the Papacy during the Mongol expansion
events.description: In 1256, Pope Alexander IV (r. 1254–1261) was responding to the news of Mongol expansion and the fall of Alamut. The Papacy was attempting to understand the Mongol threat and to determine whether the Mongols might be potential allies against Islamic powers or a threat to Christendom itself. In 1256, the Papacy was still in the early stages of diplomatic contact with the Mongol Empire, and the full extent of the Mongol threat to Christian Europe was not yet apparent.
events.certainty_note: The Papacy's awareness of Mongol expansion in 1256 is documented in papal correspondence, though specific diplomatic initiatives in that year are less precisely recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Alamut's destruction as a purely destructive event has been complicated by modern scholarship. While the fortress was destroyed, some sources suggest that Hulagu's forces preserved some of the Assassins' library and archives, and that the destruction was not as complete as traditionally portrayed. Additionally, the Assassins' reputation as purely terrorist assassins has been challenged by modern historians, who emphasize their sophisticated theology, administrative structure, and political role. The characterization of the Assassins as a threat to Islamic civilization is partly a product of later Islamic historiography, which emphasized their heterodoxy and their use of assassination as a political tool.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1256 is moderate for major political and military events (fall of Alamut, establishment of the Il-Khanate, English baronial tensions, Song-Mongol conflict) but heavily biased toward court-sponsored chronicles and religious narratives. Islamic sources (Rashid al-Din, Juvayni) provide detailed accounts of the Mongol conquest of Persia, though written decades later. European sources focus on political and ecclesiastical events. Chinese sources document Song-Mongol conflict but with less precision for individual years. Economic, social, and cultural developments outside the elite sphere are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The fall of Alamut enabled Hulagu's establishment as Il-Khan
The destruction of the Assassins in 1256 enabled Hulagu's campaign against Baghdad in 1258
Hulagu's reign as Il-Khan lasted from 1256 to 1265
Baronial tensions in 1256 escalated into the Barons' War in 1264
Song military campaigns in 1256 were part of the two-decade struggle against Mongol conquest
Papal awareness of Mongol expansion in 1256 was heightened by the sack of Baghdad in 1258
